title: Royal Golden Peanut Banana Pockets
description: Crisp golden diamond-cut pockets with a layered filling of smooth peanut paste and soft banana — light sugar dusted and served warm. A clean, rich, and elegant sweet snack.

tips: Keep the flame on low throughout. The goal is aromatic, not browned. Overcoasting turns it bitter.
subSteps: Place a small pan on low flame.
Add the desiccated/dry coconut powder.
Dry roast for 1–2 minutes, stirring constantly.
Stop as soon as it smells aromatic — it should stay light in color.
Transfer to a plate and let cool completely before using.

tips: Use just-ripe bananas — not overripe. Overripe bananas release too much moisture and make the dough soggy.
Keep the mash slightly chunky for texture contrast inside.
No sugar in the banana mix — sugar draws out moisture and will make the pocket leaky during frying.
subSteps: Peel 2 just-ripe bananas.
Using a fork, lightly mash them — keep the texture slightly chunky, not smooth.
Add 2 tbsp of the roasted coconut powder.
Add a pinch of cardamom (yalakki) powder.
Add a tiny pinch of salt.
Mix gently until just combined.
Set aside in a small bowl. Do not refrigerate.

tips: Grind the peanuts while still slightly warm for a smoother paste.
The paste should be thick and spreadable — like peanut butter. If too dry, add a few more drops of warm ghee.
This peanut layer acts as a moisture barrier between the dough and banana filling, keeping the pocket crisp.
subSteps: Measure ½ cup of pre-roasted peanuts.
Grind peanuts in a mixer/blender to a fine, almost powdery texture.
Warm 1–1.5 tsp ghee slightly (just warm, not hot).
Add warm ghee to the ground peanuts.
Add a pinch of salt.
Add 1–2 tbsp powdered sugar.
Mix well until it forms a thick, spreadable paste — similar in consistency to thick peanut butter.
Taste and adjust sweetness if needed.
Set aside covered.

preparation.instruction: Make the dough and roll into discs.
tips: Use slightly less water than a standard chapathi dough — a firmer dough holds the filling better and results in a crispier fried shell.
Rest the dough for at least 5 minutes after kneading so the gluten relaxes and it rolls evenly.
Roll discs slightly thicker than a chapathi — too thin and they will burst during frying.
subSteps: Measure 1.5 cups wheat flour into a mixing bowl.
Add a small pinch of salt.
Add water gradually (approximately ½ cup), mixing as you go.
Knead into a smooth, slightly firm dough — it should not be sticky.
Cover the dough and let it rest for 5–10 minutes.
Divide the dough into 8 equal portions.
Roll each portion into a disc approximately 3 inches in diameter.
The discs should be slightly thicker than a chapathi — not paper thin.
Keep unused discs covered with a damp cloth to prevent drying.

ingredients.name: Water
ingredients.notes: use less than usual — slightly firm dough

ingredients.name: Ghee
ingredients.notes: 1 tsp for peanut paste, rest for frying — use more as needed

ingredients.name: Powdered Sugar
ingredients.notes: 1–2 tbsp in peanut paste, rest for final dusting

ingredients.name: Salt
ingredients.notes: divided — pinch in banana filling, pinch in peanut paste, pinch in dough

tips: The layered structure — peanut, banana, peanut — is key. The peanut paste acts as a moisture barrier on both sides, keeping the dough from turning soggy.
Seal edges very firmly with no air pockets. Any gap will cause the pocket to burst open during frying.
Do not overfill — a thin, even layer of filling fries better and holds the shape.
subSteps: Take one rolled disc and place it on a clean flat surface.
Spread a thin, even layer of peanut paste across one half of the disc — leave a 0.5 inch margin at the edges.
Place a small amount of banana mixture in the center on top of the peanut layer.
Spread another thin layer of peanut paste on top of the banana filling.
The structure from bottom to top should be: dough → peanut → banana → peanut.
Fold the disc over to form a half-moon shape.
Press the edges firmly together, making sure no air is trapped inside.
Crimp or press with a fork along the sealed edges for a tight, secure closure.
Repeat for all 8 discs.
Keep assembled pockets covered while you work.

tips: Cutting before frying gives cleaner diamond edges and more even frying.
If you prefer, fry whole and cut immediately after frying while still warm — the filling stays contained better this way.
A sharp knife or pastry cutter gives the cleanest diamond cuts.
subSteps: Take each sealed pocket and flatten it gently and evenly with your palm.
Using a sharp knife or pastry cutter, cut diagonally to create diamond shapes.
Each half-moon disc typically yields 2–3 diamonds depending on size.
Re-seal any cut edges that have opened up by pressing firmly.
Lay cut diamonds on a flat surface, not stacked, to keep their shape.

steps.instruction: Fry the diamonds to golden crisp.
tips: Slow frying on medium-low flame is essential — the banana filling needs time to heat through and the peanut paste needs to set without burning the outer shell.
Ghee gives a richer flavor than oil. Use a mix if ghee quantity is a concern.
Do not rush with high heat — the inside will remain raw while the outside burns.
Fry in small batches so the oil temperature stays consistent.
subSteps: Heat ghee or oil in a deep pan or kadai on medium-low flame.
Test oil readiness by dropping a tiny piece of dough — it should rise slowly and sizzle gently.
Gently slide in 3–4 diamond pieces at a time. Do not overcrowd.
Fry on medium-low heat, turning occasionally, for 4–6 minutes per batch.
The pockets should turn an even golden color on all sides.
Flip gently using a slotted spoon to avoid breaking the sealed edges.
Remove when evenly golden and place on a plate lined with kitchen paper.
Let rest for 30–60 seconds before finishing.

steps.instruction: Dust with powdered sugar and serve.
tips: Dust while the pockets are warm so the sugar adheres lightly without melting completely.
Arrange in a neat diamond pattern on the serving plate for a premium presentation.
subSteps: While the fried pockets are still warm (not piping hot), dust generously with powdered sugar.
Use a fine sieve or strainer for an even, light dusting.
Arrange on a serving plate in a neat diamond layout.
Serve immediately while warm for the best contrast of crisp shell and soft filling.

seo.metaTitle: Royal Golden Peanut Banana Pockets — Diamond Cut Sweet Snack
seo.metaDescription: Crisp diamond-cut fried pockets layered with smooth peanut paste and soft banana, finished with a light sugar dust. An elegant Indian sweet snack.
